glossaryTerms.definition: A rod or cylindrical former around which clay is shaped to produce a ring or tube form; a pen barrel works as a simple mandrel for small rings.
A surface pattern created by pressing a textured object into soft clay to leave an imprint of its surface.
The process of hardening polymer clay by baking it in a domestic oven at the temperature specified by the manufacturer, typically 110-130 degrees C.

content.text: Rings made around a
mandrel
are one of the most straightforward polymer clay projects: roll a slab, add
impressed texture
, cut a strip, wrap it around a pen barrel, and bake. The ring holds its shape on the mandrel during
curing
and slides off cleanly once cool. A set made in coordinating colours looks put-together and takes about 30 minutes to make. These rings are decorative pieces only.

tools.name: Pottery rolling pin or smooth glass bottle
Pen barrel or similar cylindrical mandrel
Fettling knife or craft knife for trimming
Texture tool: lace, leaf, fabric, or pottery stamp
Baking tray lined with greaseproof paper

content.text: Preparation

Warm the clay in your hands until soft. Measure the circumference of the pen barrel you are using and add 5 mm to allow for the join overlap. A standard pen barrel is around 35-40 mm in circumference. Measure your ring size beforehand: a standard UK size L or US size 6 corresponds to an inner diameter of roughly 16.5 mm. Choose a pen barrel or similar cylinder that matches your target inner diameter. Preheat the oven to 110-130 degrees C.

Roll the clay into a flat slab about 2-3 mm thick. The slab should be at least as wide as the ring will be tall (10-15 mm is a comfortable ring band height) and long enough to wrap around the mandrel.

While the slab is flat, press the texture into the surface. Place a piece of lace, leaf, or fabric face-down on the clay and press with your fingers, then roll the rolling pin once firmly across to ensure even contact. Peel the texture material away slowly. Alternatively, press a stamp or textured object firmly and lift straight up. The impression should be clear and consistent across the whole slab.

Cut a straight-edged strip from the slab using the fettling knife and a ruler. The strip should be the height you want the ring to be, typically 10-15 mm, and about 5 mm longer than the mandrel circumference to allow for overlap.

Wrap the strip around the pen barrel with the textured side facing out. Press the two ends together firmly where they meet and smooth the join on the inside of the ring with your fingertip. Trim any excess so the ends meet cleanly without bunching.
Stand the pen barrel upright on the lined baking tray. Bake at 110-130 degrees C for 20-25 minutes. The ring stays on the mandrel during baking; this keeps it perfectly round.
Remove from the oven and allow to cool fully before sliding the ring off the pen barrel. Pulling a hot ring will deform it. Once cool, it slides off with a gentle twist.
Variations
Use two contrasting colours rolled into a two-tone slab for striped texture effects.
Make a wider statement ring by increasing the band height to 20-25 mm and using a bolder texture.
Make a set of three rings in graduated widths from the same textured slab for a stacking set.
After curing, coat with a thin layer of varnish to bring out the texture and add a slight sheen.

items.problem: Ring is oval rather than round after baking
items.cause: Ring shifted on the mandrel or mandrel was laid on its side during baking
items.fix: Bake with the pen barrel standing vertically so gravity acts evenly. Use a small piece of crumpled paper to prop it if needed.

items.problem: Join line is visible on the outside of the ring
items.cause: Ends overlapped or not trimmed neatly before joining
items.fix: Trim both ends of the strip cleanly with the fettling knife so they meet in a neat butt join, then smooth from the inside with a dampened fingertip.

items.problem: Ring will not slide off the mandrel when cool
items.cause: Clay expanded around any texture or notch on the pen barrel during baking
items.fix: Use a smooth pen barrel with no clip or ridges. A gentle twist while pulling usually releases the ring; do not force it straight off.
